Well, you know, I wish, as Clayton said, my story is a little different. I don't have a wonderful rags to riches story like Carline does and Clayton and Paris. I sort of stumbled into this world. And I was thinking, in thinking about this speech, I realized that there's really a moral to how I kind of stumbled into it, that you can really learn from, hopefully not stumbling. And you know, what it is, I think, the common thing and everything that I've done, it's just kind of like learning from the best, learning from other people, learning from people who are better than I am. And it started when I was a couple of years out of college and I was kind of looking for a job. I crashed a school bus, so I sort of lost that job of, you know, teaching in a school and driving the school bus. It wasn't that I didn't like lose any kids or anything, but it kind of damaged the school bus and it just wasn't a good thing to do. And I am like, I am a really, really fast typist. And so I said, I could do that. I could earn some money pretty quickly. And I went to a temp agency, which was at the time something like Kelly Girls. So I thought that was pretty cool that I could be a Kelly girl. And you know, they said, well, we've got an opening in the typing center. It's a place called Ogilvy and Mather. And it's an ad agency. And I'm like, yeah, whatever. Okay. I had sort of, Ogilvy and Mather sort of had a familiar ring to it, because Ogilvy was kind of famous even in the general culture. And so I went and I got a temporary position there. And I started reading the stuff that they were doing. And I thought to myself, wait a minute, I think I can do this. And so they gave me a chance to do it. And I started doing it. And I got un-tempt and permanently hired and started writing copy there. And it was, I just kind of woke up at like one of the best ad agencies in the world and probably the best ad agency when it comes to teaching the people who work there how to do advertising. Ogilvy was a fiend about, even in direct response, we quote David Ogilvy and what he did. So to actually be there where it permeated the halls, his wisdom, his writings, what he said, he sent me a note once that said, you write well. And I was like, that's one of my favorite testimonials for the rest of my life. If no one ever said anything about my writing. But he published books, he was constantly teaching. Everyone there had a, he used to give away copies of capels and Hopkins. I mean, what a great place to start. So I just had a great time being there learning from that. And it made me realize how important the learning is. So I started working at other ad agencies, became a creative director at an ad agency. And one day I was reading Success Magazine and there was this long copy ad in there from a guy named Jay Abraham. And it was all about this marketing genius thing. And he just had this, one of the things about copywriting is to be able to write stuff that just seems like, when you use common sense, it seems like it must be true. Clayton was kind of saying that's a way of proving things. You might not have like proof that you could go before a judge, but you say, doesn't it make sense that? And Jay's stuff was just like that to me. I read Jay's stuff and I'm like, my God, this stuff makes sense. The idea of leveraging marketing assets makes sense. The idea of a unique selling proposition. The idea of host-parasite relationships. Getting other companies to mail for you. Getting, you know, buying leads from your competitors. Everything he said had that ring to it. And so I decided I want to do that. I want to be like a junior Jay Abraham. And so I left the world of advertising. And Jay had written about a guy named John Finn, who was a copyright, Clayton's laughing because we all, John Finn was such a great guy. And he was a copywriter's agent in LA and I sent him some stuff and he liked it. So he took me on as a client, which meant that I got to tell people I have an agent in LA, which I just thought was so incredibly cool. And so John started finding me jobs. I started working for companies. And one day he said there's this guy named Jim Rutz. And he's looking for someone to help him write copy, to kind of be an apprentice copy cub kind of a thing. And he said, Jim's kind of a strange guy, but if he likes you, you'd really be in and you'd learn a whole hell of a lot. And so I sent Jim some stuff. And Jim is like real gruff. It's still funny. To this day, I still remember how I sent him all these samples and I got to fax them, of course, in those days. And so I called him up. I said, did you get the samples? And Jim says, yeah, I got the samples and it just kept coming. So I finally showed off the fax machine. That's Jim. It's like this just flat monitor. And the irony of it is that Jim writes the most interesting copy in the world. No one writes copy like Jim. You start reading. One of the ways of telling good copy is you read the first half of a sentence and you cover up the second half. And you see if you can guess what the second half is. And a lot of times in people's copy, you can guess. Not only do we this, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, you can never predict where Jim's going to go in a sense. He's going to bring in the Bible and the apocalypse and he's going to make a reference to Las Vegas. He's just going to go all over the place and you're fascinated by it. So I started working with Jim and I learned how not to be boring because if it was boring, Jim would just send it back. He goes, this is boring. Make this not be boring. And sometimes Jim would take the copy and he would make it not be boring. He would revise it. And so I would take what he had done and just study to death. How did he do that? How did he change my... Look what he did. He made it into this little mini story. He added color to it. He brought in this little reference to it. He made it have people doing things in it. So to this day, I still have old Jim's edits so I can study them and see what he did, how creative he was with that. So I worked with him for about a year and then after that, Jim kind of went on to doing his open church thing more and I was ready to be on my own and people were ready to hire me because I not only... One of the things about working with someone like that, not only do you learn from them, but there's a little bit of a rub off reputation effect. Oh, you worked with Jim Rutz. And boardroom knew me from working with Jim, Phillips knew me and so they hired me right away to do things. And so I started working with the big mailers that we always talk about and even while doing that, I would take like a year off to go work with Dan Rosenthal for a year, who Clayton mentioned. And I learned so much from working with him. He's the master of the eight page sales letter, which I love because I just hate writing like 36 pages. And he can write eight page sales letters. It's bringing millions of dollars in business. And his specialty really was being able to disarm you. You never felt like you were being sold. You just thought he was explaining things and he would say, use the rapier. Don't use the sword. He would just sting you with the rapier in his copy. Very personal. It always had personality to it. It was always someone... Some copy is, we do this and we do this and blah, blah, blah, and blah, blah, blah. And Dan's copy is always... I was talking to a friend of mine about whatever. There's always a soul behind his copy. He once did a crit for me. I'll never forget. He said, this is soulless copy. And he was right. There was no... I was talking about travel. And I was writing about, you can do this and you can do this and blah, blah, blah. And he took it and he had me turn it into... Friend of mine went to Disneyland recently and he was able to get in, not waiting in line because of this, blah, blah, blah, and blah, blah, blah. And one time I went here and did this and did this. And all of a sudden the copy came to life. But anyway, that's what I worked with Dan. As Clayton said, Ben Zivanga worked with Dan. Jay Abraham worked with Dan many years ago. And then Ben Zivanga, he worked... Not only did he work with Dan, he worked at Ogilvy like I did. Plus, Ben Zivanga also worked with Caples. Yeah. So, I mean, no wonder he's so good. He learned from the best. So even to this... And then I went down and worked with Gary Halbert on the Key West seminars and did some copy for people there and John Carlton kind of supervised that. And that was just a tremendous experience and a great time from what little I can remember of my nights in Key West with Gary Halbert and John and Gary North with the dreadlocks and everything. That was pretty amazing. And so I did that. And then for a year seems to be my pattern. So I did that. I did that. I worked with Dan for a year. And I spent a year working for my friend Bill Royal who had a direct response agency. But I wanted to be on the inside like that. I wanted to work for a direct response company and kind of learn the mechanics of direct response from the inside. And he wanted me to be creative director. So it was kind of an interesting challenge to me to actually have to manage people and manage a creative department. And as Marcella will no doubt tell you, any help that I can get in terms of managing people is probably very useful. So I did that for a year. I got to work with Jay, you know, not after admiring him from a distance for so long. I got to go to LA for a week and work with Jay in his offices, which was just tremendous to be with Jay for like 10 hours a day and see how he thinks and see how. Because that's the thing really, you know, you can read about these things. I think it's a trap in a certain way. You know, Clayton was saying, we used to have all this stuff that's available. And it's great that we do. And it is. But in a way, it's a little bit of a trap because you think it's not complete. And it's not complete until you work with someone like Clayton or you work with Jay or you work with someone who's better than you in any way and you see how they think. It's not just the rules they go by. You see the thought process. Like, you know, with John Carlton, I saw the way he approaches and ad. I saw the way he kind of circles the problem. I saw the way John has this way of taking a marketing problem and he's going to write an ad and he'll turn it into this one paragraph of, you know, like for golf or something like that. And before he writes, he'll have this thing in his head of something like, you know, I know that you're god damn sick of your friends beating you at golf and, you know, you're ready to drive that ball, you know, this or that. And you're tired of all these courses that don't do this and don't do that. Just this really curmudgeony kind of way in his head. And I saw how he does that and how he thinks. So I think that's what you get from that. And from having someone read your copy and review your copy, throw it out when it's not good, kill your darlings. It's just so important to your growth as a copywriter. So I guess that's, you know, and to this day, I still have a lot of people in this room I give my copy to to read and we do that for each other. I know I've had Gary Benzavenga critique my copy and that's something I'll never forget. And I guess that's what I really want to convey more than anything else is any opportunity that you have to write copy for someone that, you know, that you admire that's a better copywriter than you to work in a situation where you do that to write renewal notices for them to do anything that you can, you know, work in the typing center of Ogilvy and Mather is just such an opportunity that you should really just, you know, seek out in any way you can. You know, there's a Marcella. I'm just trying to wake up here is Marcella was this great thing where Marcella was at this boot camp and Mike Palmer gets up and, you know, he says, I've got this opportunity. We're looking for people to come and work with me in in Baltimore. And you know, you start at like a really low salary and, you know, we teach you about the writing and stuff and there's a chance to make royalties and there's a chance to make big money, you know, really big money. And you know, clearly no one in the room was interested. We're copywriters. We love our freedom. We ain't working in Baltimore. We ain't working for the man. You know, we're just, we're just not doing it, you know. And so Marcella bless her heart, gets up there, takes the mic and all but screams at them. You friggin idiots. You have a chance to work with the guy that created the the end of America campaign that brought in like a billion dollars for Agora probably brought in a small fortune from Mike himself. You have the opportunity to be paid to study under this man and learn from this man and learn from being at Agora and then to make royalties and make a whole lot of money there and then to go out on your own eventually and make like a million dollars a year. And you're like, I know why they moved to Baltimore. It's cold after that. I just, I'm on my own. I just, and she really just read them the riot act. And you know, she was incredibly right. You know, any opportunity like that, whether it's a year working for them or whether it's just like, you know, pay somebody $500 to critique your ad that's a writer that you admire. Anything you can do to do that just turbocharges your growth in a different way than you can than reading a book or even going to a seminar. And that's why at these seminars like Clayton says, show your stuff to people. You know, show your stuff to the writers here, to me, to whoever and get the feedback and be grateful if it's like harsh feedback. If someone tells you this is boring as heck, you know, if Jim told me my copy was boring as heck, I would soap was so happy because it was an opportunity for me to learn how to make copy that wasn't boring. And it was an opportunity for me to not pretend that my copy was interesting when it wasn't. And that's what makes a great copywriter is the ability to just to know when your copy writer, when your copy is not interesting. So, you know, that's why I, that's why I'm here at this seminar is to learn. And I just urge all of you to just never stop learning.
